annotations.label: Linear equation

anchor.snippet: a linear equation is an equation that may be put in the form

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated 'linear equation' object; the left-hand side a₁x₁+…+aₙxₙ+b is an affine functional captured by AffineMap, but the equation/zero-set itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Unique solution in one variable

anchor.snippet: In the case of just one variable, there is exactly one solution

annotations.note: No packaged linear-equation solver, but bijectivity of multiplication by a nonzero field element (Equiv.mulLeft₀) yields the unique solution of ax+b=0.

annotations.label: Solutions form a line (two variables)

anchor.snippet: The solutions of a linear equation form a line in the Euclidean plane

annotations.note: Lines are modeled as 1-dimensional affine subspaces (and Collinear is defined), but there is no theorem that a two-variable linear equation's solution set is such a line.

annotations.label: Solutions form a hyperplane (n variables)

anchor.snippet: the solutions of a linear equation in n variables form a hyperplane

annotations.note: Rank–nullity and LinearMap.ker give that a nonzero functional's kernel has codimension one, but the affine zero-set being a hyperplane is not packaged as a statement.

annotations.label: Linear equation in one variable

anchor.snippet: A linear equation in one variable x can be written as

annotations.note: The map x ↦ ax+b is an affine map k→k (AffineMap), but the one-variable linear equation is not a defined object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solution of one-variable equation

anchor.snippet: The solution is

annotations.note: The formula x = -b/a is not a named lemma; it follows from inverting multiplication by the nonzero coefficient (Equiv.mulLeft₀).

annotations.label: Linear equation in two variables

anchor.snippet: A linear equation in two variables x and y can be written as

annotations.note: ax+by+c is an affine functional on k² (AffineMap), but the two-variable linear equation has no dedicated formalization.

annotations.label: Infinitely many solutions

anchor.snippet: it has infinitely many solutions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theorem that a nondegenerate two-variable linear equation over an infinite field has infinitely many solutions.

annotations.label: Unique solution for y defines a function

anchor.snippet: It therefore has a unique solution for y

annotations.note: Solving for y when its coefficient is nonzero is an instance of inverting nonzero multiplication (Equiv.mulLeft₀); the resulting function-of-x is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Linear function (graph is a line)

anchor.snippet: The graph of this function is a line with slope

annotations.note: Mathlib defines slope of a function (slope_def_field: (f b - f a)/(b - a)) and affine maps, but not 'linear function y=mx+c whose graph is a line' as a packaged definition.

annotations.label: Affine functions and linear maps

anchor.snippet: the functions whose graph is an arbitrary line are often called affine functions

annotations.note: Mathlib precisely distinguishes affine functions (AffineMap) from linear maps (LinearMap/IsLinearMap in Mathlib.Algebra.Module.LinearMap.Defs), matching this terminological definition.

annotations.label: Correspondence between lines and equations

anchor.snippet: all solutions of the equation form a line

annotations.note: Lines exist as affine subspaces, but the bijective correspondence between lines and (classes of) two-variable linear equations is not formalized.

annotations.label: Vertical line case (b = 0)

anchor.snippet: the line is a vertical line

annotations.note: The coordinate-specific notion of a vertical line x = -c/a is not a concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Horizontal line case (a = 0)

anchor.snippet: one has a horizontal line of equation

annotations.note: The coordinate-specific notion of a horizontal line y = -c/b is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Slope–intercept form

anchor.snippet: A non-vertical line can be defined by its slope m , and its y -intercept

annotations.note: Mathlib has a slope function and affine maps but no dedicated slope–intercept representation y=mx+c of a line.

annotations.label: Point–slope form

anchor.snippet: of any point of the line

annotations.note: slope_def_field rearranges to the point–slope relation y - y₁ = m(x - x₁), but point–slope form is not itself a defined representation.

annotations.label: Intercept form

anchor.snippet: does not pass through the origin cuts the axes into two different points

annotations.note: The intercept form x/x₀ + y/y₀ = 1 of a line is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-point form (unique line)

anchor.snippet: there is exactly one line that passes through them

annotations.note: lineMap and affineSpan give the line through two points (collinear_pair shows two points are collinear), but the ∃!-uniqueness of the line through two distinct points is not packaged as a theorem.

annotations.label: Determinant form

anchor.snippet: can be expressed simply in terms of a determinant

annotations.note: Mathlib has collinearity criteria but no determinant-form equation of the line through two given points.

annotations.label: Linear equation in more than two variables

anchor.snippet: A linear equation with more than two variables may always be assumed to have the form

annotations.note: a₁x₁+…+aₙxₙ is a linear functional (LinearMap) and with constant an affine map, but the n-variable linear equation is not a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Constant term

anchor.snippet: is called the constant term

annotations.note: 'Constant term' here is terminology for b in a linear equation and has no dedicated formalization (only loosely related to polynomial constant coefficients).

annotations.label: Solution as an n-tuple

anchor.snippet: A solution of such an equation is a n -tuple such that

annotations.note: The generic notion of a solution as an n-tuple satisfying the equation is not formalized as a specific definition for linear equations.

annotations.label: Meaningfulness condition

anchor.snippet: the coefficient of at least one variable must be non-zero

annotations.note: The condition that at least one coefficient be nonzero (i.e. the functional is nonzero) is not stated as a meaningfulness lemma for linear equations.

annotations.label: Solutions are points of a hyperplane

anchor.snippet: are the Cartesian coordinates of the points of an

annotations.note: Affine subspaces and codimension-one kernels (via rank–nullity) exist, but there is no theorem identifying the solution set with a hyperplane.

annotations.label: Solving for a variable defines a function

anchor.snippet: the equation can be solved for x j

annotations.note: Solving for xⱼ when its coefficient is nonzero is an instance of inverting nonzero multiplication (Equiv.mulLeft₀), yielding an affine function (AffineMap), but this is not stated for linear equations.
